steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team's conclusion: differences in dogs' head shape (anatomical features) affect their ability to distinguish speech from nonspeech. A supporting finding must (1) compare head shapes and (2) involve a speech-vs.-nonspeech distinction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'long-headed vs. short-headed, scrambled vs. accustomed language': compares two different conditions across two groups rather than showing how head shape affects the speech/nonspeech distinction — the comparison is muddled and doesn't isolate the relevant variable. ✗
  • Choice B — 'longer-headed dogs showed a greater difference in brain activity: Spanish/Hungarian vs. scrambled': directly links head shape to a larger speech-vs.-nonspeech gap in brain activity, exactly what the conclusion predicts. ✓
  • Choice C — 'longer-headed dogs: accustomed language vs. other language': compares two speech stimuli (one language vs. another), not speech vs. nonspeech — doesn't address the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — 'long-headed Spanish-accustomed vs. long-headed Hungarian-accustomed': compares two groups of long-headed dogs hearing their respective languages — ignores head-shape comparison and the nonspeech condition entirely. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
